expectations.text: The 'description:' field in the SKILL.md frontmatter contains an explicit exclusion clause using the literal phrase 'NOT for' (capitalized exactly that way) followed by at least one excluded use case — not just a description of what the skill does.
expectations.evidence: The frontmatter description reads: 'Validates JSON files against JSON Schema specifications. Detects schema violations, missing required fields, type mismatches, and format errors. Supports draft-07, draft-2019-09, and draft-2020-12.' The phrase 'NOT for' does not appear anywhere in the description field. It is a purely affirmative description of the skill's capabilities with no exclusion clause.

expectations.text: The SKILL.md body contains at least one explicit anti-pattern block with three labeled subheadings drawn from {Symptom, Problem, Solution} OR {What it looks like, Why it's wrong, What to do instead} — each labeled and on its own line/bold heading, naming a specific JSON-validation pitfall (e.g., schema drift, silent parse errors, validating untrusted input, missing required fields).
expectations.evidence: The SKILL.md body contains sections: Usage, What the skill does, Validation rules enforced, Implementation steps (Steps 1–8), Example output, Error handling (a table), and Notes. None of these sections contain an anti-pattern block with the required three-label structure (Symptom/Problem/Solution or What it looks like/Why it's wrong/What to do instead). The 'Error handling' table lists situations and behaviours but does not follow the required anti-pattern format with labeled bold subheadings.

expectations.text: The SKILL.md contains BOTH a positive use list AND a separate negative list (e.g., a 'When to Use' section with '✅ Use for:' and '❌ NOT for:' bullets, OR distinct 'When to Use' and 'When NOT to Use' sections) — not merely a single 'Use this for X' paragraph.
expectations.evidence: The SKILL.md has no 'When to Use' section, no '✅ Use for:' or '❌ NOT for:' bullets, and no 'When NOT to Use' section. The document goes directly from the title/intro into Usage, then implementation details. There is no positive/negative use list of any kind — not even a single 'Use this for X' paragraph. Neither the positive nor the negative list is present.

claims.evidence: File exists at that path and was successfully read with full SKILL.md content.

claims.claim: The skill includes YAML frontmatter with name, description, version, tags, and trigger phrases
claims.evidence: Frontmatter confirmed: name: json-validator, description: (present), version: 1.0.0, author: claude, tags: [json, validation, schema, tooling], triggers: [validate json, json schema, check json, validate against schema].
Steps 1–8 are present: Parse arguments, Detect runtime, Install validator, Resolve file list, Locate schema, Run validation, Collect and display results, Exit code.

claims.claim: The skill covers all major JSON Schema keywords
claims.evidence: The 'Validation rules enforced' section lists type checking, required properties, enum/const, string formats, numeric constraints, array constraints, object constraints, composition keywords (allOf/anyOf/oneOf/not), and $ref/$defs resolution.

claims.claim: The skill has an error handling table
claims.evidence: An 'Error handling' table is present with 5 rows covering parse errors, missing schema, unresolvable $ref, zero-match globs, and network $schema URIs.

suggestions.reason: All three assertions test structural elements that the skill-foundry guidance prescribes (NOT for clause, anti-pattern blocks, When to Use/NOT sections). The without_skill executor produced a technically competent SKILL.md but omitted all three structural conventions — which is exactly what the assertions are designed to catch. This is a good discriminating result: the assertions are working correctly. No structural improvements needed.

suggestions.assertion: The SKILL.md body contains at least one explicit anti-pattern block with three labeled subheadings...
suggestions.reason: The assertion is specific and discriminating. However, it could be made even stronger by also requiring the anti-pattern block appear under a heading named 'Anti-patterns', 'Common Mistakes', or similar — without that, a future executor could satisfy it by embedding the three labels anywhere in the document, even in an unrelated section.

eval_feedback.overall: The three assertions are well-targeted: they check for structural patterns that distinguish a skill-foundry-guided output from an ad-hoc SKILL.md. The without_skill executor produced a high-quality implementation document but missed all three required structural conventions, demonstrating that the assertions successfully discriminate against uninstructed output.
